sentences.text: The quasi Jewish center, as we would call it in English, was really not to service or enhance Jewish life, but was to serve the Germans with a documentation as to the residents, the occupation, or whatever Jews might be doing.
Because, you see, in Germany we had a system, a police system, a police state that is not known United States.
Number one, in order to be able to live in a city, Gentiles and Jews alike had to register with the police.
So Germans always knew where everybody was-- by address, by location, or by workplace.

Number two, if you wanted to go to vacation somewhere-- you wanted to visit your grandparents-- if it was more than one week, you had to de-register in the city where you live and re-register in the city you were visiting.
So the Germans knew where everybody was every minute of the day-- or every day that they were I guess you might say at home, unless it was a less than seven day vacation trip.
The Jewish center had the responsibility of doing the same thing that the police did.
They had to know where everybody was.
And with this particular facility that we had to go and pick up the yellow star.
Oh, you hear both by rumors and by connections that you did keep with your friends and your families.

And because you were interested all this time to leave the country, to leave Germany, to emigrate to any country in the world that would have you, you kept in touch with the Jewish center because they provided information as to where you might have entry and how to get there.
But most of the time, of course, it was your own individual effort to try to escape Germany.

Consequently, the problems of registration with the police, which of course you never did anything to disturb that-- the only time that we had to go to the police was when Germany forced Jewish males to add the name Israel to the name and the Jewish women were forced to add Sarah to the name.
And since this has to become your legal name, you had to go to the police department and register.
And believe me, you went with a great deal of apprehension.
Nobody can understand what it's like to go into a beehive of Nazi officials, especially police, and hopefully just be able to change your name and sign it and then walk out of it.
One cannot understand the German mentality, what it is to officially, patriotically, as they would call it, hate and abuse Jews.
Anything they wanted to do, they got away with it.
There was no policemen.
There was no court.
There was no judge, no Supreme Court that would uphold the rights.

On the contrary, you would see many times judges sitting in courts, and what they would display with a great deal of pride is the party pin, the party insignia, and there was no way that you could ever find justice.
So consequently, this Jewish service center provided us with information and with updates.
And also, of course, that's the place where you have to pick up the star.
You could not pin that on.
The reason was the Germans would assume that if you pinned it on, you could take it off and slide by a get by, as we would say, would pass as a non-Jew.
So we have to have plenty of stars for the few clothing we had that we had to sew it on.
And you were not likely to cut it off and sew it on, and cut it off and sew it on again.
So it could not be pinned on.

That's how we got the star, and of course from then on you were obviously totally I guess you might say a sore in even a walking population because you just stood out with an insignia on back and on your chest.
And of course that intimidated everybody not to go anywhere unless you absolutely had to.
I don't think anybody can understand-- unless you are perhaps racially different and can be recognized as being like none of the majority-- what it's like to have to be so labeled and so marked.
And then have supposedly tried to live not a normal life, but just a, a meager existence.
In 1939 and thereafter, things deteriorated even worse.
Without going into gruesome details for that--
Well, the personal experience was that I did make an attempt once to use a street car because I had to-- I wanted to, and I don't even remember what my mission was to even take the chance.
And I stood on the street car, on the steps and holding on to a rail.
It's very similar as we have transportation in San Francisco in the cable car.
You didn't take a chance to go in there, and certainly never, ever would you have the nerve or courage to sit down and take a place when a Gentile would have to stand up.
That was just impossible.
So I hung on for probably something like seven or eight blocks.
Well, it was, of course, wonderful sport for somebody on the street car to kick and try to push me off while I was riding the streetcar.
And I managed to get kicked off, but unfortunately held on with one hand.
And here I'm dangling on the street car when they decided-- a woman-- now talk about German mentality-- decided to bite my hand so I would let go and fall on the street.
Fortunately I didn't get that hurt-- hurt badly, but the incident was enough never, ever to even attempt to use anything that could possibly once again create such an incident-- for your own safety.
And nobody would ever know some of the things, the brutality.
One of the things that was also an incident that I walked in the street once, and obviously with my star, and you tried to avoid encountering any group of two or three or more youngsters.
And [INAUDIBLE] here are these blonde, blue-eyed uniformed Hitler Youth which somehow must have detected me, pursued me, and the one thing they wanted to see is what a circumcised penis looked like.
So they dragged me out and ripped my clothes off and thought that that was very funny.
I don't think it had a traumatic effect on me, but it's certainly-- you can't erase these memories of an example of what some people can do to you.
And not only get away with it, but be considered little big shot heroes.
Past 1939, with the daily and nightly air raids-- at least in Cologne-- things were now getting terribly devastated because buildings were damaged.
And that mean-- that meant fewer residences were available.
And now was the time to hopefully get Jews out and have some Gentile occupying your residence.
Of course, most of the businesses had been already taken over.
Jewish businesses didn't exist in '39 and thereafter.
Huge concerns may have existed surreptitiously, but since most of these owners of major Jewish businesses could afford to leave the country, most them by then had left.
We had 600,000 Jews out of the population of six million-- of 60 million Germans, and by World War II, that had already diminished to, I believe, far, far less than half.
I'm not quite sure.
It means that half of the Jewish population had opportunities to leave Germany by World War II.
My uncle and aunt, who had brought me to this country after the war, were one of the last ones to legitimately leave Germany in early 1939.
Thereafter, there was no way out once the war had started.
But even before, people do not understand and ask why, why didn't you just leave?
Number one, where do you go to if nobody wants you?
There was no entry.
Not the United States.
Not Canada.
The world was closed.
There were a few exceptions-- the Dominican Republic was one and Shanghai was another one.
But you had to be extremely wealthy to be afforded-- be able to afford the bribery to get in there.
Germany itself was a truly fortified border country, which meant it is not a free border.
The country itself was totally closed.
It was closed by fortification.
It was closed by machine-gun towers.
There was no way you could cross the border without getting killed.
Transportation, automobiles-- people didn't own automobiles in those days.
Airplane transportation was very meagre and simply did not exist as a mode of proper transportation.
This was the '30s.
Airplanes were obviously not available for much public transportation for another reason after '39, because they were used for war effort.
So the only thing that was available is either on foot and, yes, also by rail.
But believe me, the Germans were famous for being able to search and turn railroad cars upside down in search for and anyone that wanted to escape.
So there were very, very few escape possible.
That's why you could not leave Germany.
And, of course, with World War II in progress, there was even less of a chance even of the remote possibility of escaping.
It just-- we were stuck.
You just couldn't get out.
Did you participate in conversations with your parents about escaping or trying to escape?
Yes.
First of all, my father had a very distant relative, like a second cousin, living in the United States.

And about once a month or twice a month they would write a letter pleading to get an affidavit to allow us to-- we did have a number from the German authority and the American consulate that would allow us to get an exit visa.
You couldn't just simply say, hey, I'm gone.
You had to have a permission to leave, which the Germans didn't particularly care to give, only because they didn't want Jews to escape.
They had other ideas.
Eradication of the Jews became a major effort on the part of the Germans, especially, as I mentioned, a moment after the start of World War II, after 1939.
Because up to that point, if you did have an exit visa and a visa from-- an entry visa, you had a somewhat reasonable way of getting out.
Usually you had to go to Holland to get a ship, and the Germans reluctantly would do it because it went by the law.
It went by the rules.
But that was not very easy.

And after 1939, especially with the invasion of Poland, Germany, of course, changed everything because now they started building ghettos and started building concentration camps, which ultimately became extermination camps.
The first concentration camp in Dachau was opened four weeks after Hitler came to power in 1933.
And although the Germans always claimed that they didn't know anything about it, you don't have 13 major, major, major concentration camps all over Germany and know they don't exist.

As a matter of fact, when you asked for assistance from any German family-- as I mentioned we lived in an apartment house with Gentile families-- the standard answer is to be, I would love to help you, but I'm afraid I'll catch pneumonia.

Pneumonia was the catch phrase that the Germans always would send to the surviving family members of those that they have killed, that your dear departed have died of pneumonia in the concentration camp.
The method of killing didn't mean anything.
It was just the fact, the notification was pneumonia.
On the rare occasion, heart failure, but pneumonia was the catch phrase.
And the Germans used this.
Instead of saying, I'm afraid I'm going to end up in the concentration camp, which they claimed they didn't know anything about-- they did.
Everyone knew it.
Because any infraction and any criminal activity would end up with a confinement in a concentration camp, with a rare reappearance.
People just simply would not come back.
So pneumonia was the catch phrase.
Consequently, with the original intent to have concentration camps for internment in Germany, now after World War II this philosophy changed, and they started building ghettos.

Ghettos were usually districts of a city, major city-- Warsaw, Lodz-- that would be fenced in, electrified, into which all the Jews that they had in Poland, in Lodz, in Warsaw or ultimately from Germany, confined in an area where you had-- I believe we had in one place in Lodz 27,000 rooms with 160,000 people.
So 1939 and thereafter, things have changed in Germany because of the wartime existence and now the Jews were considered to be not only enemies, but traitors.
Because everything now changed rapidly to the possibility of sending Jews out of Germany.
In 1941, January 1941, we were told by the Jewish center that deportations will take place.
And we received a message from the German official, basically police, that you have to report, let's say, on Monday at 8 o'clock in a huge facility.
Like most cities we know would be a convention center which was empty.
And it was supposed to be filled with Jews destined to go somewhere, destination unknown.

The Germans do not rely on volunteer reporting and have always used darkness, the nighttime, including the German shepherds, including sticks, guns, rifles, butts and so on to invade your home and in the darkness of night simply grab you.
You did have your little suitcase packed because you're not allowed to take much.
Just the very minimal.
Certainly no luggage possibility.
And here we were, supposedly reporting on Monday, when on Saturday night they came and grabbed my parents and myself out of the apartment.
We were shut on a truck where other people had already been collected, and then we were delivered to this convention center.
We sat there, and we sat there for two days.
They gave us some bread on Sunday, and by the time all these people were shoved in over a two-day, day and a half period of time, they had quite a collection, the exact number which I don't know.
Well, first of all fear was one thing.
My mother still had her mother living.
My father had his mother and eight brothers and sisters living, seven of which were in Germany.
One was in Holland.
And this, this yanking away from not only your domicile but from the whole family life-- even though they didn't live in the same city-- was a near panic, at least totally confusing.
You were helpless.
You couldn't resist.
You couldn't do anything.
You couldn't run.
You couldn't hide.
It just simply did not exist that you have a way out.
I wouldn't say escape.
You just could not even not be present.
It just was not possible.
So here we were with total confusion, with many other people, as to what will happen.
Where will we go.
Nobody knew.
They shoved us on a train, and with the travel of about two days we arrive someplace and discovered upon unloading that there was nobody there who spoke German.
They all spoke Polish or some Yiddish.
And we were made to understand, finally, that we had arrived in Lodz.
Lodz was the second-largest ghetto.
And the Germans, of course, with the occupation of Poland, and they had a year and three months' time to organize that.
They had confined these 167,000 people into a very, very small say-- I don't know how you would explain it-- five, eight, 10 block area.
Actually, a little bit larger than that.
And we were shoved into a room with 14 other people.
We were originally held in a holding place which were barracks, and then assigned to this room.
And we thought, well, we'll have a place to stay.
But what the future will hold, nobody knew.
We would then told to go through this room, and that's where we can stay.
And we walked in, and you had 11 other people.
We were a total 14 people in one small room.

The room was large enough that you could crouch down, sit down, and then later we were able to disperse a little bit more as things calmed down a little bit, and we ended up with a total of five people per room.
Now room is a misnomer.
Architecture and everything in Polish cities at that time were extremely primitive.
There were no toilet facilities.
There was an outhouse.
There was no running water anywhere.
Warm water, cold water-- forget it.
That didn't exist.
Even before the war this was not the advanced architectural style of a Polish city.
And we had to go to a pump to get water, which obviously during wintertime was frozen.
So it was an existence that's very, very hard to believe.
Why were we there?
Basically, forced labor.
My father was forced to reinforce the fence that surrounded the ghetto.
And we were given so little food that in three months' time, he died of starvation.
My mother was not a very-- Were you present when he died?
Yes, right in the room that we shared with the other people.
What do you feel when your-- I don't know.
That's a very thing-- difficult thing to describe.
Seeing anybody die-- and I had seen death before all over the place-- but you own parent, it's a very difficult thing to describe.
First of all, my father died more or less by falling asleep type of thing, so there was no suffering.
He was not ill.
He just gave out.

My mother and I, of course, were totally confused by this unexpected-- although you can expect it if somebody is ailing, but when you see somebody just simply wither away, and we didn't look much different than my father, you think in a very short time thereafter whether you have the same fate.
The-- if you want to say the traumatic thing about my father's passing was the burial.
There was no burial.
The corpses were placed outside wherever you lived, onto the street, and a wagon came by in the morning collecting the corpses that had died during the night.
And you saw heaps of bodies.
You were in a confined area that did have a cemetery.
And as it turned out-- and I'll tell you in a moment-- that one on my survival possibilities was to work on the cemetery at one time.
The idea was that you had no formal burial.
They just simply took the body, wrapped it in something like a sheath of material which was much too expensive, so it was the same sheet that's like cheesecloth, very transparent.
The body hung.
The arms flung out.
And there was a pit.
And your whole ceremony of burial was that you walk to the pit and they just simply turned the sheet around and the body falls in with the others that had just gone through the same burial service--
No.
You said your own things, what you felt in your heart was all the service.
There was no service of any kind.
The-- talking about service was that when I was taken to Lodz, it was by coincident one week before my bar mitzvah.
Bar mitzvah, I had trained for secretly, not knowing where it would be held because synagogues and temples didn't exist, of course.

But every once in a while, just to uphold Jewish tradition, you would have it maybe in a-- I can't say home because we didn't have homes-- a facility where there was a large room and people, your family, would get together and you'd rush through the service.
Well, I didn't even have that.
Consequently, the holding of services, which did exist in the ghetto secretly and irregularly, and in different places because you were not allowed to congregate.
But they did have Jewish services.
They even sometimes had other activities like somebody would present some songs or whatever it is.
Culture was being maintained in a haphazard manner because the Germans were not personally present except during the day.
So at night you were just closed off so attempts were made, and if there was a shul, or anybody bar mitzvah'ed, they would do it in a big hurry.
But they did manage to have some services.
But burial services, no.
There was no-- unless you know rabbi, there was no service.
It was just done very quickly.
My mother worked in a factory about 14 hours a day making the front plates to German boots.
The military boots had metal plates that always made this intimidating sound when they marched.
And of course to make the boots last longer.
And she had to drill the holes in them.
A major opportunity for her to get an extra ration of food.
I worked 12 to 14 hours or more sometimes in an agricultural manner in plowing fields and planting for vegetables, with an additional bonus of being able at harvest time to steal food.
At one time I hid a lot of carrots, and my mother and I would share it with the people in the room, which was a rarity, believe me, that you even had enough to share.
And we ate so many carrots, including at work, that I promptly turned yellow and had jaundice from basically, a vitamin-- as we now know, a vitamin A toxic level.

And I looked like a pretty sick individual, which was a very dangerous thing to be, because when Germans inspected you and you didn't look healthy, you had no guarantee of being around the next day because various types of selection took place.
Except it wasn't organized selection.
I was there from 1941 to 1943.
Managed to get by, scrounging, stealing, eating anything.
We discovered, for example, that when I had to work on the fields, that there was a peculiar plant that was edible.
And you kept it as an absolute secret that you could eat  that.
You ate everything and anything you could find.
No, I only it as [POLISH], which is the Polish name.
The one that I do remember, which was an absolute treat, was dandelion.
It became a regular science because you ate the dandelion only before it bloomed.
Otherwise, it became inedible.
You just could not eat the incredible bitterness.
And so you looked around and you waited for a little dandelion.
The joke was also that because I worked in the agricultural capacity that I had a tiny, tiny plot of land where I could do something for myself.
And for those that worked in this agriculture on the field, the Germans gave us a little plot.
And the irony is you plant it, you nurture it and you watch it grow, and then overnight somebody else came and pulled out the potatoes or the carrots that you had.
So you never had a yield from whatever you tried to plant.
The reason that I had access to that, because in my work I had access to the seeds.

Now the most important thing, and I first, according to the German Criminal Act, was that you normally wore knickers, knickerbockers, because you had to work snow, and you only had one set of clothing.
You don't have-- you know.

The beauty of it was that you could bring your socks over the knickerbockers and make a baggy look, and I would proceed to steel beans or dried peas-- anything that would plant-- and you have to go every night through German inspection.
